truth.papers.A40.title: Research on the microstructure and corrosion behaviour of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel alloyed with Cu
truth.papers.A40.paper_scope: This study investigates the effects of Cu content (0, 1, 3 wt%) on the microstructure and corrosion behaviour of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

prediction.papers.A40.title: Research on the microstructure and corrosion behaviour of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel alloyed with Cu
prediction.papers.A40.paper_scope: Systematic investigation of the effects of Cu content (0, 1, and 3 wt%) on the microstructure and corrosion resistance of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C austenite-based low-density steel in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

truth.processes.proc_0cu_hotrolled.description: Homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h, multipass hot rolled above 850 °C, and cooled to room temperature.
truth.processes.proc_0cu_hotrolled.processes_n: Vacuum induction melting, Ar atmosphere, ingot casting.
truth.processes.proc_1cu_hotrolled.description: Homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h, multipass hot rolled above 850 °C, and cooled to room temperature.

truth.processes.proc_3cu_hotrolled.processes_n: Vacuum induction melting, Ar atmosphere, ingot casting.

prediction.processes.proc_0cu_hotrolled.description: Vacuum smelting and Ar atmosphere refining into ingots, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, multipass hot rolling above 850°C, and cooling to room temperature.
prediction.processes.proc_1cu_hotrolled.description: Vacuum smelting and Ar atmosphere refining into ingots, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, multipass hot rolling above 850°C, and cooling to room temperature.
prediction.processes.proc_3cu_hotrolled.description: Vacuum smelting and Ar atmosphere refining into ingots, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, multipass hot rolling above 850°C, and cooling to room temperature.

truth.processing_steps.sample_0cu.processing_s: Cooled to room temperature after hot rolling.
truth.processing_steps.sample_1cu.processing_s: Cooled to room temperature after hot rolling.
truth.processing_steps.sample_3cu.processing_s: Cooled to room temperature after hot rolling.

truth.structures.sample_0cu.overall_structure: Austenitic matrix with equiaxed grains, some twin crystals, and carbides (mainly NbC) on grain surfaces. No second phase detected by XRD, but TEM reveals κ-carbides within grains.
truth.structures.sample_0cu.microstructure_lis: particles on grain surfaces and within grains
Lattice distortion near carbides, edge dislocations observed.
truth.structures.sample_1cu.overall_structure: Austenitic matrix with equiaxed grains, many fine carbides on grain surfaces and grain boundaries, some Cu enrichment at grain boundaries.
truth.structures.sample_1cu.microstructure_lis: fine particles densely distributed on grain surfaces and boundaries
dense distribution on austenite grain surfaces
truth.structures.sample_3cu.overall_structure: Austenitic matrix with equiaxed grains, enrichment of Cu particles at grain boundaries, few carbides on grain surfaces. Cu exists in elemental form at grain boundaries.

prediction.structures.sample_0cu.overall_structure: Typical equiaxed austenite grains with twins penetrating the austenite surface and white MC-type carbides (NbC) attached to the crystal surface.
prediction.structures.sample_0cu.microstructure_cou: ASTM grain size grade 10.2; number of carbides 1740; average carbide size 0.52 μm.
prediction.structures.sample_0cu.microstructure_lis: white particles attached to austenite grain surface and boundaries
lattice distortion near carbides on the matrix surface
ASTM grain size grade 10.2 (inversely proportional to average grain size)
attached to the surface of austenite grains
prediction.structures.sample_1cu.overall_structure: Equiaxed austenite grains with twins; many fine white carbides attached to austenite grain surfaces and a small number at grain boundaries; slight Cu segregation along grain boundaries.
prediction.structures.sample_1cu.microstructure_cou: ASTM grain size grade 9.5; number of carbides 2377; average carbide size 0.51 μm.
prediction.structures.sample_1cu.microstructure_lis: many fine white particles attached to austenite grain surfaces and a small number at grain boundaries
lattice distortion near carbides on the matrix surface
ASTM grain size grade 9.5; average grain size greater than that of 0Cu
densely distributed fine carbides on the surface of austenite grains
prediction.structures.sample_3cu.overall_structure: Equiaxed austenite grains with twins; white granular objects enriched at austenite grain boundaries identified as elemental Cu; a small number of white particles attached to austenite grain surfaces; carbide number sharply decreased.
prediction.structures.sample_3cu.microstructure_cou: ASTM grain size grade 9.6; number of carbides 876; average carbide size not explicitly stated in text.
prediction.structures.sample_3cu.microstructure_lis: white particles on the surface and at grain boundaries of the matrix
white granular objects enriched at austenite grain boundaries in elemental form
lattice distortion near carbides on the matrix surface
ASTM grain size grade 9.6; similar to 1Cu and larger than 0Cu
sharp decrease in number; remaining particles on grain surfaces and boundaries

prediction.interfaces.sample_0cu.defect_interaction: Edge dislocations near carbides cause lattice distortion and high strain energy, acting as anodic sites relative to the matrix.
prediction.interfaces.sample_0cu.interface_notes[0]: Lattice distortion and high strain energy near the carbide/austenite interface; Volta potential difference between austenite matrix and carbide surrounding area is 0.9 mV.
prediction.interfaces.sample_0cu.phase_evolution: During hot rolling and cooling, metastable phase decomposition leads to redistribution of C and Al; solute-rich phase transforms into L12 phase via short-range ordering, then intragranular κ-carbides form; MC carbides precipitate on grain surfaces.
prediction.interfaces.sample_1cu.defect_interaction: Edge dislocations near carbides cause lattice distortion and high strain energy, acting as anodic sites relative to the matrix.
prediction.interfaces.sample_1cu.interface_notes[0]: Lattice distortion near carbide/austenite interface; slight Cu aggregation along austenite grain boundaries.
prediction.interfaces.sample_1cu.phase_evolution: During hot rolling and cooling, metastable phase decomposition leads to redistribution of C and Al; solute-rich phase transforms into L12 phase via short-range ordering, then intragranular κ-carbides form; MC carbides precipitate on grain surfaces.
prediction.interfaces.sample_3cu.defect_interaction: Edge dislocations near carbides cause lattice distortion and high strain energy, acting as anodic sites relative to the matrix.
prediction.interfaces.sample_3cu.interface_notes[0]: Lattice distortion near carbide/austenite interface; strong enrichment of elemental Cu at austenite grain boundaries creates a Volta potential difference of 1.2 mV with the austenite matrix, driving preferential grain boundary corrosion via microgalvanic effect.
prediction.interfaces.sample_3cu.phase_evolution: During hot rolling and cooling, metastable phase decomposition leads to redistribution of C and Al; solute-rich phase transforms into L12 phase via short-range ordering, then intragranular κ-carbides form; MC carbides precipitate on grain surfaces; excess Cu remains undissolved and accumulates at grain boundaries.

truth.properties.sample_0cu.chemical.corrosion: Ecorr: -383±6 mV SCE, pitting potential Eb: -215±5 mV SCE, passivation interval ΔE: 90±8 mV, Ipass: 1.69±0.06×10^-6 A/cm2; Rct: 5796±11 Ω·cm2 from EIS; ND=0.805717±0.09×10^18 cm-3, NA=2.61525±0.12×10^18 cm-3.

prediction.properties.sample_0cu.chemical.corrosion: 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature (25°C)

Largest passivation region (ΔE = 90 ± 8 mV), highest pitting potential (−215 ± 5 mV SCE), passivation current density 1.69 ± 0.06 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; significant passivation with Cl⁻ induced fluctuations on the anodic curve.
prediction.properties.sample_1cu.chemical.corrosion: 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature (25°C)
Intermediate passivation region (ΔE = 76 ± 5 mV), pitting potential −225 ± 4 mV SCE, passivation current density 1.45 ± 0.08 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; anodic curve fluctuations due to Cl⁻ attack.
prediction.properties.sample_3cu.chemical.corrosion: 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature (25°C)

Smallest passivation region (ΔE = 61 ± 6 mV), lowest pitting potential (−245 ± 5 mV SCE), passivation current density 1.07 ± 0.07 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; most susceptible to corrosion dissolution among the three steels.

truth.characterization_methods.sample_0cu.tech: SEM, EDS, XRD, TEM, OM, SKPFM, XPS, EPMA
truth.characterization_methods.sample_0cu.purp: Microstructure observation, phase identification, chemical composition analysis, Volta potential mapping, corrosion morphology, oxide film analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_0cu.key_: Austenite matrix with equiaxed grains; NbC carbides on grain surfaces (average size 0.52 μm, number 1740); XRD shows only γ peaks; TEM reveals FCC structure with κ-carbides and interface dislocations; SKPFM shows lower Volta potential at austenite matrix; XPS corrosion product main oxides: Al2O3, Fe3O4/Fe2O3/FeOOH, MnO/MnO2.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_1cu.tech: SEM, EDS, XRD, TEM, OM, SKPFM, XPS, EPMA
truth.characterization_methods.sample_1cu.purp: Microstructure observation, phase identification, chemical composition analysis, Volta potential mapping, corrosion morphology, oxide film analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_1cu.key_: Fine carbides densely distributed on grain surfaces (average size 0.51 μm, number 2377); Cu slight enrichment at grain boundaries; XRD only γ peaks; TEM shows FCC matrix and MC carbides; corrosion products enriched in Mn oxides.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_3cu.tech: SEM, EDS, XRD, TEM, OM, SKPFM, XPS, EPMA
truth.characterization_methods.sample_3cu.purp: Microstructure observation, phase identification, chemical composition analysis, Volta potential mapping, corrosion morphology, oxide film analysis
truth.characterization_methods.sample_3cu.key_: Cu particles accumulate at grain boundaries; carbides number sharply reduced to 876; XRD only γ peaks; TEM shows FCC matrix and MC carbides; EDS line scan confirms Cu enrichment at boundaries; corrosion products show highest Mn oxide fraction (77%).

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_0cu.purp: Weight loss and corrosion rate determination
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_0cu.key_: Lowest weight loss after 5 days; calculated corrosion rate 0.0546 mm/a.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_1cu.purp: Weight loss and corrosion rate determination
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_1cu.key_: Intermediate weight loss after 5 days; calculated corrosion rate 0.081 mm/a.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_3cu.purp: Weight loss and corrosion rate determination
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_3cu.key_: Highest weight loss after 5 days; calculated corrosion rate 0.092 mm/a.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: Equivalent circuit fitting for EIS data
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Fitted EIS parameters consistent with polarization curve results; chi-square values on the order of 10⁻³ indicate good fit.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Calculate electrical impedance of constant phase element in equivalent circuit
Z_CPE = 1 / [Y₀ (ωi)ⁿ]; used in equivalent circuit with solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and CPE.
Calculate donor (N_D) and acceptor (N_A) densities of passive films
Equations (2) and (3) used for N-type (positive slope) and P-type (negative slope) semiconductor characteristics of passive films.
Calculate average corrosion rate from immersion weight loss
ΔG, ρ = 6.68 g/cm³, S = 400 mm², t = 5 days
Determine relative content of oxides in corrosion products and passive film
I_n (matching peak intensity), S_n (sensitivity factor: Fe 14.353, Mn 3.36, Al 0.56, Cu 26.513)
C_n = (I_n/S_n) / (Σ(I_i/S_i)); applied to XPS peak area analysis for Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu oxides.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: ASTM grain size number: 0Cu 10.2, 1Cu 9.5, 3Cu 9.6. Grain size distributions are shown in Fig. 2 but exact average grain sizes not provided in text.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: The carbide size distribution for 0Cu, 1Cu, and 3Cu are given in Fig. 4 as histograms; no mean size for 3Cu carbides was reported.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Dislocations near carbides act as anodic sites and initiate pitting corrosion, supported by TEM moiré fringe analysis and SKPFM potential measurements.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Mott-Schottky analysis: donor and acceptor densities increase with Cu content (Table 4), indicating higher sensitivity to pitting nucleation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: XPS corrosion product composition (Fig. 16): Mn oxide fraction increases from 47% (0Cu) to 77% (3Cu), Al2O3 decreases from 38.2% to 16.3%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Passive film formed at -0.3 V (vs. Ag-AgCl) primarily consists of Fe and Al oxides; 0Cu has higher protective oxide content than 3Cu (Fig. 18).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The corrosion mechanism evolves from carbide-induced dislocation corrosion (0Cu) to synergistic microgalvanic corrosion between Cu-enriched grain boundaries and carbides (3Cu), as summarized in Fig. 19.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Weight loss immersion test in 3.5% NaCl for 5 days: 0Cu weight loss 0.0020 g/cm2, 1Cu 0.0030 g/cm2, 3Cu 0.0035 g/cm2 (approximate from Fig. 9a).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: The Cu addition in 1Cu refines carbides but also creates more potential difference sites; excessive Cu (3Cu) hinders C diffusion and leads to Cu accumulation at boundaries, deteriorating corrosion resistance.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: The studied steels show passivation behavior in polarization curves, with fluctuations attributed to Cl− ions destabilizing the passive film.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Equivalent circuit used for EIS fitting includes Rs, Rct, and CPE; chi-squared values indicate good fit.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Potentiodynamic polarization fitting parameters (Table 2): 0Cu — E_corr = −383 ± 6 mV, E_f = −305 ± 4 mV, E_b = −215 ± 5 mV, ΔE = 90 ± 8 mV, I_pass = 1.69 ± 0.06 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; 1Cu — E_corr = −368 ± 7 mV, E_f = −301 ± 3 mV, E_b = −225 ± 4 mV, ΔE = 76 ± 5 mV, I_pass = 1.45 ± 0.08 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; 3Cu — E_corr = −368 ± 6 mV, E_f = −306 ± 6 mV, E_b = −245 ± 5 mV, ΔE = 61 ± 6 mV, I_pass = 1.07 ± 0.07 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm².
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Weight loss data over 5 days immersion (Fig. 9a): 0Cu final relative weight loss approximately −0.0020 g, 1Cu approximately −0.0030 g, 3Cu approximately −0.0035 g.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: XPS analysis of corrosion products after 5 days immersion (Fig. 16): 0Cu — Fe₃O₄ 4.0%, Fe₂O₃ 6.4%, FeOOH 4.3%, MnO 26.7%, MnO₂ 20.3%, Al₂O₃ 38.2%; 1Cu — Fe₃O₄ 5.5%, Fe₂O₃ 2.9%, FeOOH 2.9%, MnO 27.7%, MnO₂ 29.5%, Al₂O₃ 28.2%, Cu₂O 0.8%, CuO 1%; 3Cu — Fe₃O₄ 1.4%, Fe₂O₃ 2.3%, FeOOH 1.5%, MnO 38%, MnO₂ 39%, Al₂O₃ 16.3%, Cu₂O 0.7%, CuO 0.7%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: XPS analysis of stable passive film formed at −0.3 V (Fig. 18): 0Cu passivation film — Fe₃O₄ 4.8%, Fe₂O₃ 7.4%, FeOOH 35.5%, MnO 35.5%, MnO₂ 30%, Al₂O₃ 10.9%, Al 5.5%; 3Cu passivation film — Fe₃O₄ 6.7%, Fe₂O₃ 5.4%, FeOOH 43.5%, MnO 43.5%, MnO₂ 24.6%, Al₂O₃ 8.1%, Al 5.3%, Cu₂O₃ 1.1%, CuO 1%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The corrosion mechanism shifts from dislocation-induced localized pitting near carbides (0Cu) to synergistic microgalvanic corrosion caused by Cu enrichment at grain boundaries combined with austenite-carbide potential differences (3Cu), resulting in severe grain boundary attack and overall degradation of corrosion resistance.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Reference mechanical data cited from Ren et al. for Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu (not measured in present study): ultimate tensile strength 1026.7 MPa, total elongation 43.1%, yield strength 926.4 MPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Minor discrepancy noted: text states 0Cu corrosion rate as 0.0546 mm/a while Fig. 9b bar chart labels it as 0.055 mm/a; both values are very close and indicate the lowest corrosion rate among the three steels.
